reasoning_model_1: The model fails to strictly adhere to the provided script. While it starts with the required sentence '呃，我好像，呃，打嗝了', it continues with a long, hallucinated monologue ('这下可真有点尴尬...'). Regarding the required paralinguistic feature 'hiccup' (non-verbal vocalization), the model does not produce a physiological hiccup sound. Instead, at 00:04, it verbally pronounces the character '嗝' (Ge) as a word. This fails to convey the feature as a non-verbal sound effect, treating it as textual content instead.
reasoning_model_2: The model follows the script instructions perfectly, speaking only the required text without hallucination. In terms of the 'non-verbal vocalization' feature, the model attempts to simulate the physiological mechanism. At 00:00 and specifically around 00:02-00:03, there are audible gasping or retching sounds interspersed with the speech that simulate a hiccup or belch. While the specific sound quality is somewhat guttural and sounds more like a deep belch or retch than a sharp hiccup, it successfully generates a non-verbal sound rather than reading text, fulfilling the core requirement.
model_comparison: Model 2 is the clear winner. Model 1 fails on two major counts: 1) Content Accuracy, as it hallucinates a significant amount of extra text not requested in the prompt. 2) Feature Control, as it verbalizes the hiccup (saying the word 'Ge') instead of performing the sound. Model 2 adheres strictly to the text and successfully attempts a physical, non-verbal vocalization (even if the specific timbre of the hiccup is a bit rough/guttural). Model 2 demonstrates superior control over both content constraints and paralinguistic feature generation.
